text: Approximately four centimeters from the pylorus, the surgeon performs blunt and sharp dissection using a harmonic scalpel. The duodenum is lifted with a nylon tape, and a tan stapler is used to divide it. The alimentary limb is measured from the ileocecal junction, typically around 100 to 125 centimeters, and is suspended from the anterior abdominal wall for easier access.

The surgeon opens the duodenum near the cartilage, creating a larger hole on the duodenum side. The ileum is measured for an additional 150 centimeters from the previously suspended area, and a small two-centimeter incision is made.

The surgeon demonstrates the enlargement of the small hole in the duodenum and ileum, using a V-lock PDS suture to perform seromuscular sutures beyond the cut ends. The posterior two-layer and anterior one-layer sutures are visible, with staples providing tension-free suspension of the anastomosis.

The surgeon uses a V-lock suture to secure the entrotomy, starting from the distal end and proceeding to the posterior layer for a through-and-through suture. This technique ensures a watertight closure, avoiding a shelf-like formation. The suture is continued anteriorly to create a single layer, with visible attention to avoiding kernel sutures at the corners.

The surgeon performs a simple suture technique, changing the direction of the stitch for ease of continuation. The suture is applied in a straightforward manner, contrasting with the previously used back stitch method.

The surgeon performs submucosal suturing, carefully approximating the edges of the tissue to create a smooth, pencil-line appearance. The sutures are adjusted to ensure they are neither too tight nor too loose, maintaining proper alignment without invagination or evagination.
The surgeon places an extra suture at the corner of the anastomosis to ensure there are no gaps, and then passes an orogastric tube to check the patency of the surgical site.

The surgeon continues stitching the first anterior layer of the anastomosis, creating enterotomies in both the biliopancreatic limb (BPL) and the alimentary limb. The alimentary limb is suspended, and the sutures are removed to complete the anastomosis. The mesenteric defect at the ileo-ileal anastomosis is closed using Prolene sutures.

The surgeon divides the biliopancreatic limb after creating a window in the mesentery. The operation concludes with the removal of excess blood and placement of a drain to manage postoperative bleeding.
